candidate_problems.text: What concrete, non-circular criterion distinguishes 'record-formation' or 'knowledge-instantiation' from generic entropy-increasing correlation growth? The conjecture risks redescribing the thermodynamic arrow in epistemic language unless it specifies when a physical correlation counts as a durable record, why such records are asymmetric under time-symmetric laws plus a low-entropy boundary, and whether this adds explanatory content beyond standard statistical mechanics.

Why was there a one-sided low-entropy boundary at all, rather than a generic state, a two-boundary universe, or no global temporal asymmetry? This is the deepest unresolved tension because the conjecture relocates the arrow-of-time problem from dynamics to cosmological initial conditions without yet explaining the origin, selection, or necessity of that special boundary.

Can the conjecture be made empirically discriminating against rival accounts of temporal asymmetry? For example, does treating the arrow as arising from accessible-state boundary conditions plus record-formation yield novel predictions about cosmology, memory asymmetry, retrodiction, Boltzmann-fluctuation scenarios, or gravitational entropy that differ from standard Past Hypothesis formulations? Without such discriminants, it may be an interpretive reframing rather than a new explanatory theory.
